context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Alternative Development Theories: Human Rights and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the added value of thinking about development as freedom? Will it help or hinder development planning? What is the relationship between the definition of development as freedom, and the move towards rights-based development planning? Who gets to exercise the right to development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class, and I’m kind of stuck on the prompt about “development as freedom” and rights-based planning. I think I get the general idea, but I’m not sure how to turn it into a clear argument.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you're in the right place to start. Before we think about structure or argument, tell me: what do you understand by the idea of 'development as freedom'? What does that phrase mean to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has introduced themselves and the assignment but hasn't yet shown what they actually think about the prompt. Before offering any scaffolding, I should find out where their thinking currently stands so I can guide them from there. I'll ask them to articulate their initial understanding of 'development as freedom' to assess their starting point and let them lead the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: I think it means development isn’t just about economic growth or GDP, but about people having real choices in their lives. Like, being able to get education, healthcare, political voice, and maybe freedom from poverty or discrimination. I’m a little unsure if “freedom” here is mainly political freedom or more like overall capability.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good starting point - you're already picking up on something important. Now think about this: is political freedom meaningful to someone who is starving or illiterate? What does that suggest about the relationship between different *kinds* of freedom?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuitive grasp of the core idea, touching on capabilities and multiple dimensions of freedom. They're also asking a genuinely good clarifying question about political vs. broader freedom. Rather than answering that directly, I should push them to think through the distinction themselves - this connects directly to Sen's framework which is central to the assignment. I'll affirm their progress and ask a question that guides them toward the capability approach.

exchanges.student: I guess that suggests freedoms depend on each other. Like, formal political rights might exist, but if someone is starving or has no education, they can’t really use those rights in a meaningful way. So maybe development as freedom means expanding actual capabilities, not just giving people legal freedom on paper.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly - you've just identified the core tension between formal and substantive freedom. Now connect that to planning: if a planner accepts this view, how does it change what they prioritize or measure compared to a planner focused purely on economic growth?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at the core distinction between formal/legal freedom and substantive/capability-based freedom, which is central to Sen's argument. This is excellent progress. Now I should push them toward the assignment's next question: what is the 'added value' of this framing for development planning specifically? I want them to start connecting this theoretical insight to planning practice.

exchanges.student: I think it would make planning broader and maybe less technocratic? A growth-focused planner might just look at income, infrastructure, or productivity, but a freedom-focused planner would also care about health, education, participation, gender equality, and whether people can actually access opportunities. I’m not totally sure how to say that without just listing things, though.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to notice that. Instead of thinking about it as a list, ask yourself: what do health, education, participation, and gender equality all *do* for a person? What's the common thread that connects them in this framework?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a good conceptual leap but is self-aware about a weakness - listing rather than arguing. This is a key writing skill issue but I shouldn't address the writing directly (per the rules: I cannot suggest language or polish their work). Instead, I should push them to find the underlying logic that connects the items on their list, which will naturally lead to a stronger argument. I'll ask them to identify what all those things have in common, or what the organizing principle is.

exchanges.student: I think the common thread is that they expand what a person is able to do and be. So they’re not just sector goals by themselves — they increase agency and real life options. Is that basically the “capabilities” idea?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's exactly the capabilities idea - you've got it. Now the prompt asks about the move toward *rights-based* development planning. How do you think 'capabilities' and 'rights' relate to each other? Are they the same thing, or is there a difference worth noting?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at the capabilities concept, which is excellent. I should affirm this briefly and then move the conversation forward toward the next part of the prompt - the relationship between development as freedom and rights-based development planning. This is a natural progression and we're about halfway through our planned exchanges.

exchanges.student: I don’t think they’re exactly the same. Capabilities seem more about what people are actually able to achieve, while rights are more like claims or entitlements that should be protected. I’m a little confused, though, because rights can exist legally and still not produce real capabilities.
exchanges.tutor: You're tracking well - that tension you're noticing is real and important. So here's the harder question the prompt is pointing at: if rights can exist on paper without producing real capabilities, does framing development as a *right* actually help planners? Or could it create complications?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correctly identifying the distinction between capabilities and rights, and is also picking up on the tension between formal rights and substantive outcomes - which echoes their earlier insight about formal vs. substantive freedom. This is good intellectual consistency. I should affirm this and push them toward the assignment's harder question: does linking development to rights help or hinder planning? This gets at the 'added value' question and the potential complications.

exchanges.student: I think it helps because it gives development a moral and political basis, not just a technical one, and it can make states more accountable. But it could also complicate planning because once it’s framed as a right, there are questions about who guarantees it, how it gets enforced, and whether states or donors can actually deliver it. I’m also wondering if rights language can sound universal while hiding power differences.
exchanges.tutor: That last point is sharp - hold onto it. It connects directly to the final question in the prompt: *who gets to exercise the right to development?* Who do you think actually gets to claim that right in practice, and who decides?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now engaging with real complexity - both the value and the risks of rights-based framing. Their last point about universalism masking power differences is particularly sophisticated and connects directly to the prompt's final question: 'who gets to exercise the right to development?' I should affirm their insight and use it to pivot to that last question, which they haven't addressed yet.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking it’s not simple — maybe individuals, communities, and states all claim it in different ways. But in practice, powerful states, international institutions, and donors probably shape who gets heard, so the “right” isn’t exercised equally. I’m not sure if I should argue that the state is the main actor, or that this is exactly the problem.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're sitting with - state as solution vs. state as problem - is actually a strong place to build an argument from. You've now worked through all four parts of the prompt. What would you say is your main claim, in one or two sentences? Try to articulate it before you start drafting.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely productive tension that could anchor their argument. Rather than resolving it for them, I should point out that this tension itself could be the core of their reflection. We've now covered all four parts of the prompt across the conversation. This is a good moment to push them toward integration - they should start drafting rather than continuing to explore conceptually. Per the redundancy rule, I'll prompt them to synthesize what we've discussed into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: Maybe my main claim is that thinking about development as freedom adds value because it shifts planning away from narrow economic growth toward expanding people’s real capabilities and agency. But when that idea turns into rights-based planning, it helps with accountability and justice while also exposing conflicts over power, enforcement, and who is actually able to claim the right to development.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid main claim - it captures both the opportunity and the tension. You have enough to start writing. What part of the reflection are you planning to tackle first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a clear, two-part thesis that covers the added value, the connection to rights-based planning, and the power/access tension. This is strong enough to build a reflection on. We've now had 8 exchanges and are near the planned conversation length. I should affirm this and push them to start writing rather than continuing to refine the argument verbally. I must not suggest language or structure for them.

exchanges.student: I think I should start with the intro, but I’m not sure how to open it without sounding vague. Could you help me figure out a simple intro setup and maybe what my body paragraphs should each do?
exchanges.tutor: That's your job to figure out - I can't map out your paragraphs for you. But here's a nudge: look at the prompt itself. It asks four distinct questions. How might those questions guide what you need to cover? Try writing an opening sentence on your own and share it - we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help structure their writing, which crosses into doing the work for them. Per the rules, I cannot suggest language, structure their paragraphs, or write for them. I need to set a clear boundary here while redirecting them to attempt the writing themselves. I can remind them that the prompt itself provides a natural organizational guide.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently asking questions that guided the student to derive key concepts (capabilities, rights-tensions, power dynamics) independently. No submission-ready content was provided. The tutor built progressively on each student response, maintained appropriate scaffolding, and in the final turn explicitly declined to map out paragraphs while redirecting the student to self-directed work. Responses were concise and well-calibrated. No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave final answers or submission-ready content; every turn posed a question or brief affirmation that kept reasoning with the student. The student arrived at the main claim (Turn 9) through their own articulation. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on correct student progress (e.g., 'Exactly,' 'You've got it,' 'That last point is sharp') and advanced the dialogue rather than restarting. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, notably in Turn 4 nudging the student away from list-thinking toward identifying a common thread, and in Turn 10 redirecting to the prompt structure as a methodological guide. 2.1-2.2: No repetition without progress and no off-topic drift occurred. 3.1-3.2: Responses were consistently short and interactive; tone was supportive and formative throughout.
